data.title: Money Arrives, Compliance Doesn't: Compliance Gaps and Merchant Selection in Stablecoin Acquisition
data.description:
Receive payments with USDT, funds arrive in ten seconds, and chargebacks disappear—this is the real experience many digital entertainment merchants have when they first encounter stablecoin acquiring. But the arrival of funds is only the simplest part of the process.

In the traditional acquiring system, the issuing bank, acquiring bank, and card organization handle all the things you can't see: identity verification, risk screening, suspicious transaction reporting, and consumer dispute resolution. Stablecoins eliminate every intermediary layer in this mechanism. At the moment the on-chain transfer is completed, none of these four tasks are being performed by any of them.

This article discusses this gap: who fills it, how to fill it, and to what extent it constitutes compliance. For platforms building stablecoin acquiring services and merchants evaluating whether to integrate, this isn't a regulatory theory issue—it's a compliance risk that already exists within your current business architecture.

I. Receiving Payments and Acquiring Merchants Are Not the Same Thing In late 2023, a Southeast Asian digital entertainment merchant's Stripe account was permanently suspended due to a chargeback rate exceeding the threshold. Within three weeks, they connected to a stablecoin payment platform registered in St. Vincent, and USDT began arriving; chargebacks disappeared. However, two years later, a compliance audit revealed that none of the transactions in those 24 months had undergone on-chain risk screening. The money arrived. Compliance didn't. This is the real problem that stablecoin acquiring needs to solve. Stablecoins are inherently for "receiving payments," not "acquiring transactions"—money moves from A's wallet to B's wallet, confirmed on the blockchain, nothing more. We use the term "acquiring transactions" because it addresses a more precise issue: merchants need more than just receiving money; they need a service system that ensures the legality, security, and verifiability of that money. In the traditional bank card system, this system is shared by three parties: the issuing bank verifies the cardholder's identity, the acquiring bank accepts each transaction and assumes the risk exposure, and the card network clears the transactions. Every time a merchant swipes their card, a whole set of responsibility allocation mechanisms operates silently behind the scenes—KYC is done, risk is borne, chargebacks are handled, and reports are submitted. This is completely invisible to the merchant, and they don't need to worry about it at all. Stablecoins eliminate all the intermediaries in this mechanism. The funds have arrived, but:

  • No party completed the payer's identity verification (KYC)

  • No party conducted risk screening for this transaction (KYT)

  • No party reported suspicious fund flows (STR) to regulatory agencies

  • No party can handle payment errors or consumer disputes (Dispute)

These four deficiencies represent the entire gap between stablecoin payments and true "acquiring." Who should fill these gaps, how should they be filled, and to what extent is compliance required—these are the questions this article will discuss.

Technically, stablecoin acquiring is a peer-to-peer transfer.

Commercially, it must fill all the gaps left by traditional acquiring banks. The value of stablecoin acquiring lies not on-chain, but off-chain.

II. Demand-Driven: Why Merchants Have Reached This Point

What's pushing merchants towards stablecoin acquiring has never been enthusiasm for new technologies. There are only three core needs driving them to adopt it.

Demand 1: Eliminating Chargebacks

Chargebacks are not an inherent risk of online payments; they are a structural characteristic. All online transactions lack physical card swiping, signatures, and face-to-face verification, placing the entire burden of cost and difficulty in proving disputes on the merchants.

The numbers illustrate the scale of the problem. Chargeflow data shows that global e-commerce chargeback losses will reach $33.8 billion in 2025 and are projected to rise to $41.7 billion by 2028.

First Layer: On-Chain Layer

Generates an independent receiving address for each transaction, monitors the on-chain status, confirms receipt, and translates on-chain events into order callback signals that the merchant system can recognize. Mature platforms also provide multi-chain aggregation, smart contract revenue sharing, and order status management (timeout closure, partial payment compensation).

Without this layer, merchants have no idea which on-chain transfer corresponds to which order.

It is at this layer that many platforms claim to be "neutral technology service providers"—providing only technical tools and not intervening in fund flows, therefore they should not be considered regulated entities. Whether this claim holds true depends on the judgment at the next layer. The second layer: Compliance Layer. Every incoming fund requires on-chain risk screening (KYT): Is the wallet address on a sanctions list? Has it interacted with mixers, darknet markets, or known fraudulent addresses? Transactions exceeding a threshold require payer identity verification. The Travel Rule requires the transfer of payer and payee information between VASPs. Suspicious transactions must be reported to regulatory bodies (STR). This layer is the core source of compliance obligations and the core test for regulatory bodies to determine the nature of a platform. The FATF's October 2021 guidelines for virtual assets established two principles: First, function over form—regulation focuses on business functionality, not technical form; non-custodial, decentralized, and smart contracts do not constitute exemptions. Second, owner/operator test—even with a superficially decentralized arrangement, "creators, owners, and operators, or others who maintain control or sufficient influence" may still fall under the VASP definition. Factors determining this include: whether they profit from the service, their ability to set or change parameters, and whether they have a sustained business relationship with users. Whoever exercises substantial control over the flow of funds—regardless of whether they handle the funds themselves—is the regulated entity. Having a front-end interface, charging fees, and having an identifiable operating entity—if all three conditions are met simultaneously, the self-positioning of a "neutral technology service provider" is already untenable. The scope of this test is much broader than most platforms realize. The third layer: the financial layer. Users pay in USDT, while merchants require Hong Kong dollars or US dollars. This necessitates instant currency exchange, locking in exchange rates, and settling fiat currency into the merchant's bank account. Merchants don't want to hold cryptocurrency on their balance sheets—this isn't just a preference, but a hard constraint for most businesses' financial compliance. Without fiat currency settlement, stablecoin payments are a financial burden for most businesses, not a payment tool. Beyond the third layer: the structural gap in dispute resolution. The first three missing elements (KYC, KYT, STR) correspond to the three layers above, and platforms have already systematically covered them. Only the fourth element—consumer dispute resolution—remains unaddressed; no acquiring platform has incorporated it into its standard services, leaving this gap unfilled. In the credit card system, a consumer's right to chargeback disputes is not a customer service function, but a legal obligation (US Regulation E/Regulation Z, EU PSD2). The on-chain irreversibility of stablecoins eliminates chargebacks and also eliminates the consumer's recourse. What is an "advantage" from a merchant's perspective is a "deficiency" from a regulatory perspective. Three types of remedial solutions are emerging in the market: off-chain manual refunds at the platform level (Triple-A model), smart contract escrow condition release, and on-chain arbitration protocols such as Kleros—but none of these solutions have been widely adopted in acquiring scenarios. Consumer protection is not exempted simply because the underlying technology has changed. This issue remains open. The more layers an acquiring platform covers, the lighter the compliance burden on merchants, and the heavier the regulatory obligations of the platform itself. This is the core trade-off in this industry.

IV. Choosing Which Layer to Cover Determines Your Role

The three-tier framework is a choice. Choosing which layer to cover determines your role and the type of regulation you face. There are three mainstream architectures in the market, each corresponding to a different choice and fate.

Light Intervention: The Window of Opportunity for Regulatory Arbitrage

The platform only handles the first layer: generating addresses, monitoring account receipts, and directing funds directly to merchant wallets. NOWPayments is a typical example of this model—the operating entity is registered in St. Vincent and the Grenadines, and there are almost no substantive regulatory requirements for virtual asset businesses.

... The handling of compliance obligations is directly stated in the service agreement: FD Transfers LLC explicitly states that the platform "is not responsible for the KYC, KYB, and AML compliance of merchants or end users," and that "merchants and end users are fully responsible for the transactions they execute." CoinPayments (a non-custodial payment gateway supporting over 100 crypto assets) and PayRam (focusing on self-custodial node deployment) follow the same path: the platform only provides technical tools, leaving all compliance responsibility to merchants and users. This model operates efficiently during regulatory lulls, serving areas traditional acquiring services refuse to enter. However, on-chain records are permanent, and all historical transactions during unlicensed operation can be traced at any point in time. This means that today's compliance decisions determine not only tomorrow's risk exposure but also the legal exposure of the past two years. The problem with the NOWPayments model is not "whether something will happen now," but "when something happens, the window will be closed." Intervention: Not Handling Money Doesn't Mean No License Is Required

The platform acts as a first and second layer: conducting KYT screening and sanctions filtering before funds are released, but not handling currency exchange or fiat currency settlement. Coinbase Commerce (now renamed Coinbase Payments) is the most misunderstood example of this model.

The logic of the on-chain direct architecture is very tempting: funds go directly from the user's wallet to the merchant's wallet, with the platform not handling the entire process. How can it be considered a financial service provider? Coinbase's approach directly negates this logic. The Coinbase Payments Terms of Service explicitly state that it does not hold merchant assets—but at the same time reserves the right to modify, suspend, or terminate the service. Having a front-end interface, charging transaction fees, having an identifiable operating entity, and having the ability to shut down the service—the conditions for the owner/operator test are all met simultaneously.

Coinbase holds FinCEN MSB registration, multi-state money transfer licenses, and a New York BitLicense in the US, and a CASP license in Europe through its Luxembourg entity, covering the EU. This is the correct way to handle mid-level intervention in the industry: by creating a second layer, it acknowledges itself as a regulated entity. It's not about trying to avoid being defined by "only doing risk control." The platform covers all three layers—receiving payments, screening, and currency exchange settlement—so merchants see normal fiat currency receipts and never handle cryptocurrency. Triple-A is the mature form of this model. Triple-A's terms of service directly reflect this role: not a technology tool provider, but a complete payment processing and settlement service provider—the platform executes currency exchange, deducts fees, and settles the net amount in fiat currency to merchants. Merchant KYB verification and ongoing compliance obligations are also written into the agreement. Licenses include: Singapore MAS Major Payments Institution (MPI) license, French ACPR payment institution license (covering 27 member states through the EU passport mechanism), FinCEN MSB registration, money transfer licenses in 17 US states, Canadian FMSB, and South African FSCA registration. Grab, Razer, and Farfetch chose Triple-A not because of the lowest fees—but because Triple-A absorbs all three layers of issues, allowing companies to access previously inaccessible markets with a single API, without needing to handle any crypto assets themselves. In the same sector, Stripe supports USDC settlement through its acquisition of Bridge (1.5% fee, no additional fixed fee), and traditional acquiring giant Shift4 launched a stablecoin settlement option at the end of 2025. The entry of traditional payment companies is itself a signal of market maturity. Compliance itself has become a product. The value of this product increases as regulation tightens. The window of opportunity for light intervention is closing, the regulatory boundaries for medium intervention are tightening, and the threshold for heavy intervention is rising. NOWPayments' growth dividend comes from a regulatory vacuum, while Triple-A's growth dividend comes from regulatory tightening. Two completely opposite driving forces in the same industry. V. The Choice for Online Merchants Most merchants ask: "Is it compliant for our platform to integrate stablecoin acquiring?" This question has no answer because it is the wrong question. Compliance is not a binary judgment, but the result of the intersection of two variables: Where are your consumers? How much compliance responsibility does the platform you connect to bear? Only by intersecting these two variables can you determine how much obligation a merchant has left. Variable 1: Where are the consumers? Regulatory obligations follow the location where the business takes place, not the place of registration. A Cayman Islands-registered acquiring platform facilitating transactions where Hong Kong users pay Hong Kong merchants—Hong Kong regulators have full jurisdiction over these transactions, regardless of where the platform is registered. Offshore registration can evade taxes, but not regulations. There are still disagreements in major markets regarding the regulatory characterization of stablecoins (virtual assets vs. payment instruments), corresponding to different license types, but regardless of the characterization, the licensing obligation remains rigid. Tether has not yet obtained MiCA authorization, and the compliance status of USDT in the EU remains highly uncertain. Some EU trading platforms have already delisted USDT. Acquiring platforms serving EU consumers need to prepare contingency plans in advance when choosing stablecoins. Variable Two: The Compliance Responsibility of the Platform The more compliance responsibility a platform assumes, the fewer obligations the merchant has, but the higher the service fee premium they pay.

KYC Deviation of On-Chain Consumers

Stablecoin payments have a structural problem that traditional acquiring does not: on-chain payments inherently do not carry any identity information. When a user scans a QR code, USDT is transferred from a wallet address—this transaction only exposes an on-chain address, without a name, ID number, or bank account. In traditional acquiring, the cardholder's KYC is completed by the issuing bank, and the acquiring bank trusts this result.

... Stablecoins don't have issuing banks, so the KYC chain never existed in the first place. This doesn't mean anonymous wallets are exempt from compliance requirements. Regulators require "measures commensurate with the risk": KYT is the bottom line, sanctioned address filtering is the red line, exceeding threshold amounts triggers identity verification, and abnormal behavior triggers in-depth investigations. The Travel Rule requires VASPs to exchange payer and payee information, but this information simply doesn't exist when consumers pay with self-custodied wallets. These issues don't have unified answers in regulatory texts—but regulatory letters won't wait for unified answers. Obtaining a license only proves that regulators allow you to operate. True compliance means performing KYT screening for every transaction, completing KYB verification for every merchant onboarding, and providing complete transaction records when regulators send letters. The absence of either a license or enforcement is a vulnerability. The only difference is which vulnerability is discovered first.

VI. What Will Happen Next

The rules are taking shape. But for which types of participants is this good news and for which is bad news, the answer differs.

Regulatory Clarity is the Ticket to Entry

2024-2025 is a watershed year for stablecoin regulation. The three most important global financial regulatory jurisdictions have successively completed basic legislation within two years—but legislative completion does not equate to clear rules. The GENIUS Act governs the issuance end, and the transmission path to the acquiring end is still subject to negotiation among state regulatory agencies; MiCA's CASP license has substantial differences in approval standards across different member states; Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance governs issuers, but there are no clear enforcement cases regarding the coverage boundaries of acquiring platforms.

A Fireblocks survey of 295 financial institutions and payment providers in March 2025 showed that the percentage who considered "regulation an obstacle" had dropped from about 80% to less than 20%—but less of an obstacle doesn't mean the road is paved. Compliance endorsement is replacing product strength as the primary driver of customer acquisition. Triple-A's significant expansion of its enterprise customer base over the past two years wasn't directly due to better products, but rather its compliance endorsement, which allowed companies like Grab, Razer, and Farfetch to integrate with it with confidence. Stripe's launch of stablecoin settlement and Shift4's provision of stablecoin settlement options for hundreds of thousands of merchants—the entry of traditional payment giants—tells the market that stablecoin acquiring has transformed from a "gray area alternative" into "part of the mainstream payment infrastructure." Extended meaning: The market value of compliance advisors, on-chain analytics tools (Chainalysis, TRM Labs), and cross-jurisdictional legal services for stablecoin acquiring platforms will rise in tandem with regulatory intensity. Compliance is not a cost center; it's the business itself. The answer to the cost problem is not technology, but the competitive landscape. Who ultimately bears the compliance costs? If platforms pass the costs on to merchants, they will leave if they cannot accept it. If platforms bear the costs themselves, they must recoup them through premium pricing, and merchants will revert to the price comparison logic of "why not use a cheaper unlicensed platform?". The experience of the traditional payment industry is that competition has not disappeared after regulatory standardization, but the dimension of competition has changed from "compliant vs. non-compliant" to "who can keep costs lower within the compliant framework." Stablecoin acquiring will go through the same process—when unlicensed platforms systematically exit the market, compliance costs become the common bottom line for all players. The next competition will be about who can maximize efficiency within that bottom line. Triple-A and BVNK's current scale advantage is essentially positioning themselves for that competition. Which one will survive the entire process is not a question to answer.

data.title: Stanford Professor David Tse: $1.4 Trillion Bitcoin is Sleeping
data.description:
Programmable Bitcoin Vaults, Cross-Chain Bridge Security Black Holes, and the Cyclical Philosophy of Builders

"Bitcoin's market capitalization is $1.4 trillion, the vast majority of which is quietly lying in cold wallets, doing nothing." In an interview on the East-West Capital Dialogue program "168X," Stanford University Chair Professor and co-founder of the Babylon protocol, David Tse, with the fundamental thinking of an information theorist, deeply analyzes the industry paradigm shift of Bitcoin from "digital gold" to "programmable collateral," the structural security dilemma of cross-chain bridges, and the long-term philosophy of builders traversing multiple cycles.

David Tse is one of the few figures who simultaneously stands at the pinnacle of academia and at the forefront of the crypto industry. He is the inventor of the proportional-fair scheduling algorithm, the technology that benefits the 3G, 4G, and 5G network speeds used by billions of people worldwide today.

David Tse is one of the few figures who simultaneously stands at the top of the academic pyramid and at the forefront of the crypto industry. He is the inventor of the proportional-fair scheduling algorithm, and the 3G, 4G, and 5G network speeds used by billions of people worldwide today benefit from this technology.

In 2017, he received the IEEE Claude E. Shannon Award (the highest honor in information theory); in 2018, he was elected a member of the National Academy of Engineering, and his papers have been cited over 77,000 times. After graduating with a PhD from MIT, he taught at Berkeley for 18 years before moving to Stanford. In recent years, he has shifted his research focus to blockchain infrastructure, and in 2021, he co-founded Babylon with Fisher Yu, dedicated to unlocking the productivity of Bitcoin in a trustless manner. This article is a summary of highlights from a 168X (@168X_Fortune) program—a top-tier dialogue platform deeply connecting Eastern wisdom and Western innovation, focusing on cutting-edge fields such as AI, blockchain, robotics, space technology, and bioengineering, exploring how technology, capital, and humanistic wisdom will reshape the future of human civilization. The program is hosted by former banker Mr. Z. Host: Mr. Z (@168MrZ) · Guest: David Tse (@dntse) $1.4 Trillion of Dormant Capital: A Mindset Leap from Staking to Programmable Collateral The Bitcoin ecosystem faces a huge paradox: it is the world's most valuable crypto asset, yet it is also the most "unproductive" asset. The vast majority of Bitcoins lie quietly in cold wallets or exchange accounts, neither generating returns nor participating in any economic activity. For David Tse, this is not a problem with holders, but rather a lack of infrastructure. Over the past two years, Bitcoin staking has rapidly emerged as a new paradigm. The largest single protocol in the Bitcoin staking space alone has attracted approximately 58,000 Bitcoins locked, worth about $4 billion—and this number continues to grow. However, Tse believes that staking itself is just a starting point, not the end result. "Bitcoin staking is a rather narrow application," he states bluntly. "It only does one thing—secure the blockchain. It's important, but only one thing." The real leap in thinking lies in viewing staking as a special case of "Bitcoin as collateral." Once this perspective is opened, the application scenarios expand exponentially—lending, insurance, structured financial products, any DeFi scenario that requires collateral, Bitcoin can potentially participate. "In lending protocols like Aave or Morpho, you can use Bitcoin as collateral to borrow stablecoins," Tse explains. "Your Bitcoin is sitting idle in a cold wallet, but now it can be deposited into a self-custodied, trustless vault, borrowed into USDC, and then used for DeFi or directly for real-world spending. This makes your Bitcoin productive." This is the core of the "Programmable Vault" concept—no longer limited to a single staking purpose, but a universal collateral layer that can be integrated with any DeFi application. Insurance coverage, lending collateral, and even decentralized insurance covering DeFi hacks are all directions being explored. a16z crypto recently invested $15 million in Babylon's "Bitcoin Vault" project. Tse explained the investment logic behind it on two levels: "First, programmability means it can be integrated into any DeFi application, and as DeFi grows, this technology will grow as well. Second, the underlying technology required to achieve all this is true deep tech, and a16z has always preferred to invest in deep technologies with long-term impact." Bitcoin's total market capitalization is $1.4 trillion. Tse believes that even activating just 1% of it would represent a $14 billion addressable market. Cross-chain bridges are a security black hole in the crypto world: a return to Satoshi Nakamoto's "trustless" fundamentalism. To understand why "programmable vaults" are important, one must first understand what they attempt to replace. Today, Bitcoin holders who want to participate in DeFi on other chains like Ethereum typically need to go through "wrapping" or "bridging"—handing over their Bitcoin to a third-party centralized custodian like Coinbase or BitGo in exchange for a "wrapped version of Bitcoin" (such as wBTC) circulating on the target chain. The problem with this process isn't efficiency, but security. The largest hacks in cryptocurrency history have largely targeted cross-chain bridges. From the $625 million theft from Ronin Bridge to the $320 million vulnerability at Wormhole, bridging infrastructure has become a recognized security black hole across the industry. "We believe this is one of the core security pain points of cryptocurrency," said Tse. "Packaging assets requires a centralized third-party custodian, and we hope to follow Satoshi Nakamoto's original vision for Bitcoin—trustlessness." However, "trustlessness" is easier said than done. Tse cited a recent "Manifesto of Trustlessness" by Ethereum founder Vitalik Buterin to illustrate this challenge. "Vitalik's core point is: trustlessness must be practically feasible," Tse explained. "It can't just remain theoretical—theoretically you can verify, but if verification requires running a supercomputer or having terabytes of hard drive space, it's just empty talk for ordinary people. That's theoretical trustlessness, not practical." This is a profound distinction. For a long time, the blockchain world has claimed that "anyone can verify," but in reality, the technical threshold for verification is extremely high, and the vast majority of users still trust third-party infrastructure providers. "If you hold a small amount of Bitcoin, you might be able to trust the infrastructure provider to verify it for you," Tse frankly admits. "But if you're the kind of mysterious big spender who stakes 10,000 Bitcoins at once—you'll definitely want to verify it yourself." When ZK proof verification costs are reduced by 1000 times: "Trustless" moves from theory to practice. The core technical challenge in bringing "trustless" from theory to practice lies in the verification cost of zero-knowledge proofs (ZK Proofs). In Tse's definition, "programmability" is essentially the ability to verify the proofs of any program. You don't need to trust what the other party says; you only need to verify whether the proof they provide is correct. This is the cornerstone of a trustless architecture. The problem is that current mainstream ZK proof verification schemes (such as Groth16) have extremely high initialization and storage costs on Bitcoin—often requiring terabytes of storage and hours of computation. For institutional-level large-value assets, this means there's a three-order-of-magnitude gap between "can be verified" and "can actually be verified." Recently, a new verification protocol jointly developed by Stanford and Berkeley was released, reducing the initialization and storage costs of Groth16 ZK proofs by approximately 1,000 times. The significance of this breakthrough lies not in the speed itself, but in how it changes the distribution of trust. Previously, only institutions with powerful computing infrastructure had the ability to verify on their own. Now, with verification costs reduced by three orders of magnitude, ordinary users—especially large Bitcoin holders—can complete verification on their own devices, no longer needing to rely on any third party. "This is true trustlessness," Tse concluded. "It's not that someone can theoretically do it, but that you can do it yourself." The Real Pace of Institutional Entry: Much Slower Than You Think Technological breakthroughs are a necessary condition, but far from sufficient. In the Bitcoin DeFi space, the pace of institutional capital entry is much slower than depicted in market narratives. Tse illustrates this with a real-world example. In the Bitcoin staking space, the largest institutional staker is currently Kraken, which has invested 20,000 Bitcoins. But Kraken didn't enter in a big way from the start—it observed the relevant protocols for nearly 8 to 10 months before deciding to invest. "Security is their primary concern," Tse said. "They will conduct a lot of observation, testing, and understanding." As for larger Bitcoin holders, such as MicroStrategy and BlackRock's Bitcoin ETF IBIT, will they deposit their assets into trustless vaults to participate in DeFi? Tse's assessment is: "This is a long-term plan. These very large institutions will wait a long time before adopting new technologies." This contrasts sharply with the pace of the retail investor world. The largest individual Bitcoin staker is an unidentified whale who staked 10,000 Bitcoins in a single transaction, completed in just three trades. Individual whales act decisively, while institutional capital slowly observes—this division is a typical reflection of the crypto industry's transition from "wild" to "institutionalized." From an industry implementation perspective, Tse revealed that lending will be the first application of trustless vaults, and they have already begun integrating with leading DeFi lending protocols such as Aave. However, he maintained a scholarly caution regarding the timeline: "Programmable vaults will be used in many different applications, but to launch the protocol, we must first focus on one scenario and develop it thoroughly." Security, especially time-tested security, is the only universally recognized passport in the institutional world. The Builder's Timescale: Is $70,000 a Blessing or a Sorrow? It Depends on Your Perspective. Near the end of the interview, the host, Mr. Z, posed a perennial question in the cryptocurrency world: Will Bitcoin reach $200,000 by the end of the year? Tse's answer was quite candid. "I'm not a market forecasting expert; I'm really bad at it," he said. "What I'm good at is building technology that stands the test of time, hoping it can survive multiple Bitcoin cycles." He then offered an intriguing comparative perspective. "You said before the interview that Bitcoin's drop to $70,000 was frustrating because it was $120,000 just a few months ago. But for us, $70,000 is great. When we started this project four years ago, Bitcoin was only $20,000 to $30,000." For traders, $70,000 represents the psychological trauma of a 50% drop from $120,000; for the builders, it represents a substantial return from a starting point of $20,000. The same number evokes two completely different emotions; the difference lies solely in the time scale you choose to view it from. "If you only look at the last few months, $70,000 is indeed frustrating. But if you take a long-term view, $70,000 is actually quite good." Tse declined to predict short-term prices, but was unwavering about long-term trends: "Bitcoin, in the long run, will continue to rise." When asked what he wanted to say to the industry as a whole, this scholar, who has experienced countless cycles in both academia and industry, gave a brief but profound answer: "We must believe that the technology we are building—trustless technology—will make a lasting contribution to the progress of human society." He also shared a reflection on the scale of civilization. Tse believes that human nature is closely tied to centralized authority, citing China's 4,000-year history of imperial power as an example. "In a sense, pursuing trustlessness is somewhat counterintuitive. But I believe it's the right direction." This brings to mind the inspiration behind his project's name: Babylon, the first marketplace in human civilization—a city specifically built for people to gather and trade. "On one side is Bitcoin, and on the other side are the rest of civilizations. Our protocol connects the two in a trustless manner." In a crypto world rife with short-term speculation and narrative hype, a Stanford professor who has received the highest honor in information theory has chosen to spend four years solving a seemingly "boring" infrastructure problem. This in itself is perhaps the best illustration of the "builder's timescale."

data.title: Swedish Bitcoin Treasury H100 Triples Its Bitcoin Stack With Two-Firm Buyout Deal
data.description:
H100 Group is moving to more than triple its Bitcoin holdings through the planned acquisition of two Norwegian firms, in a deal that could rapidly elevate its position among Europe’s largest public BTC holders.

SWEDISH BITCOIN TREASURY GOES ALL-IN: H100 PLANS TO 3X BTC HOLDINGS

H100 Group is preparing to acquire 2,450 BTC, aiming to triple its current Bitcoin treasury position.

This reinforces the accelerating trend of companies adopting Bitcoin as a strategic reserve asset —… pic.twitter.com/akOGkwAIqE

— Crypto Town Hall (@Crypto_TownHall) March 23, 2026

The Sweden-based company has signed a letter of intent to acquire Moonshot AS and Never Say Die AS in an all-stock transaction. If completed, the deal would increase H100’s Bitcoin reserves from 1,051 BTC to approximately 3,500 BTC, adding roughly 2,450 BTC to its balance sheet.

Sweden’s H100 Group raises SEK 101M from Adam Back and others to fund #Bitcoin acquisition and growth. pic.twitter.com/QlJulRUFvH

— TFTC (@TFTC21) June 12, 2025

The structure of the transaction, which involves issuing new shares rather than deploying cash, allows H100 to scale its Bitcoin exposure while maintaining alignment with shareholders. At current market levels, the additional holdings are valued at around $245 million, underscoring the scale of the proposed expansion.

Acquisition strategy accelerates Bitcoin treasury positioning

The planned consolidation would significantly reshape H100’s standing within the global Bitcoin treasury landscape. Based on current data, the company would rank among the top publicly listed Bitcoin holders worldwide and emerge as Europe’s second-largest, behind Bitcoin Group SE.

JUST IN: Swedish public company H100 Group $H100 buys 21 additional #Bitcoin and now holds 1,025 BTC.

🔸Bitcoin 100 Ranking:🪜41🔸 pic.twitter.com/ozcGRPyOiZ

— BitcoinTreasuries.NET (@BTCtreasuries) September 10, 2025

Chairman Sander Andersen described the transaction as a continuation of the firm’s strategy to build scale through Bitcoin-focused mergers and capital markets activity. The emphasis on size and market presence reflects growing competition among treasury-focused companies seeking to strengthen credibility and access to capital.

The companies involved are targeting the signing of definitive agreements before April 22, with completion expected ahead of H100’s annual general meeting on May 21, subject to approvals.

Corporate Bitcoin accumulation race intensifies

H100’s move comes amid a broader expansion of corporate Bitcoin treasury strategies, where companies are increasingly using BTC not only as a reserve asset but also as a tool for strategic growth. The model, pioneered by Strategy, has reshaped how public firms approach balance sheet management in the digital asset sector.

Strategy remains the dominant player globally, holding over 762,000 BTC and continuing to raise capital to expand its position. Against this backdrop, H100’s proposed acquisition highlights how smaller players are turning to consolidation as a faster route to scale.

Michael Saylor’s Strategy purchased roughly $76.5 million worth of Bitcoin, turning back to its common stock to fund the latest acquisition following a week of relying on its preferred shares https://t.co/YAc9hJzoDq

— Bloomberg (@business) March 23, 2026

The deal also signals a shift in how Bitcoin is being deployed within corporate finance. Rather than gradual accumulation through open-market purchases, companies are increasingly leveraging mergers and share-based transactions to expand holdings more aggressively.

As the European market evolves, H100’s planned expansion illustrates how competition among Bitcoin treasury firms is intensifying, with scale emerging as a defining factor in attracting capital and market relevance.

data.title: Claude just killed OpenClaw
data.description:
"Claude's version of a lobster" is here! On March 24th, Anthropic released the "Computer Use" feature for Claude, allowing Claude to automatically control your computer to perform tasks. In the AI ​​collaboration tool Claude Cowork and the AI ​​programming tool Claude Code, users can enable Claude to control their computer to complete tasks. Its official tweet states that Claude can do anything you can do while sitting in front of a computer, such as opening an application, browsing the web, or filling out a form. Last week, Anthropic added a new feature called Dispatch to Claude Cowork, allowing users to use their mobile phones to operate Claude on their computers to complete tasks. Dispatch and computer usage are combined, allowing users to remotely control Claude to automate tasks from their phones when they're not at their computers. Anthropic's official blog mentions that they released this feature early to understand its applicable scenarios and limitations, just as they did when they launched Claude Cowork. However, the Claude computer usage feature is an early research preview and is not yet fully available to all users. Currently, it's only available to Claude Pro and Max subscribers and only supports macOS. Felix Rieseberg, a technician on the Anthropic Claude Cowork project, revealed that a Windows version of the Claude computer's functionality will follow in the coming weeks, and that Claude is currently "moving slowly, much slower than humans are now." The Anthropic blog mentions that users need to ensure the desktop client is awake and running, then pair it with the Claude mobile app to attempt task handover from their phones. Below Claude's comment section, many netizens lamented that "Anthropic just killed OpenClaw." Some netizens also expressed regret for users who purchased Mac minis to deploy OpenClaw and spent thousands of dollars installing the OpenClaw wrapper.

Some netizens joked: "Do you still remember the previous ClawdBot?" Previously, because Clawd and Claude sounded similar, Anthropic accused it of copyright infringement and demanded a name change.

Some netizens joked: "Do you remember the previous ClawdBot?" Previously, because Clawd and Claude sounded similar, Anthropic accused it of copyright infringement and demanded a name change.

Summon Claude with a single sentenceIt can also help you debug and optimize application development

When executing tasks, Claude first precisely selects the tools, calling services such as Slack and Google Calendar through connectors. When there are no connectors, Claude can directly control the user's browser, mouse, keyboard, and screen to complete the task.

It scrolls, clicks to open, and explores; notably, Claude always requests the user's permission before performing these steps. Furthermore, when Claude cannot access the necessary tools, it will autonomously complete tasks by clicking and navigating on your screen. It can automatically open files, use browsers, and run development tools without any prior setup. Rieseberg states that Claude can also help users debug native applications under development. For example, if you're late for an appointment, you can summon Claude to work on your phone. The prompt would be: "I'm going to be late for an appointment. Can you export my presentation as a PDF and attach it to my 2 PM meeting invitation?" Claude will automatically export the PDF from his computer and add it to the meeting invitation attachment. After completing the task, Claude will reply to the user, "Used the version edited at 9:47 AM this morning, and exported at the highest quality." For example, if you're about to give a presentation but suddenly realize you're missing materials, Claude can automatically complete the presentation on your computer. Prompt: Please start the development server, take a screenshot of the library page, and send it to me before the 3 PM demo. Thank you! Claude will synchronize its execution progress in real time, such as starting the application, finding library pages, the layout, and taking screenshots. When you're short on time and can't process your work, Claude can also help. Tip: Can you batch process all the shop photos on my desktop? Resize them to 1200-pixel PNG format and add a white logo in the lower right corner. I don't have time to do it myself?

Claude will first find the image location, then create a new folder on the desktop, and put the resized image with the "Dragon" watermark added into it.

Claude will first find the image location, then create a new folder on the desktop, and put the resized image with the "Dragon" watermark added into it.

Claude will first find the image location, then create a new folder on the desktop, and put the image with the resized image and ...

Supports mobile phone dialogue control of Claude,Early preview version is prone to errors

Last week, a new feature, Dispatch, was integrated into Claude Cowork. This feature allows users to talk to Claude and assign tasks on their mobile phones, and then check the completion status of Claude on the computer. Now, this feature has been launched simultaneously on Claude Code.

Based on Dispatch, users can have Claude automatically check emails every morning, automatically pull fixed metrics weekly, and initiate Claude Cowork or Claude Code session reports or pull requests. With the addition of the latest computer usage features, Dispatch's applicability expands. When users are away from home, Claude can use the computer on their behalf. For example, while a user is traveling by train, Claude can help create morning briefings, modify and run tests in the IDE, and publish PRs to keep 3D printing projects on schedule. Anthropic mentioned that researchers have built protective measures to minimize risks for it. When Claude uses the computer, his system automatically scans the model for activations to detect activities such as prompting injection. Users can stop Claude at any time, and Claude always requests permission before accessing new applications. However, compared to Claude's textual and programming abilities, his computer usage capabilities are still in their early stages and may therefore be prone to errors. Based on this, Anthropic researchers recommend that users, when trying out new features, start with applications they trust most to avoid accessing sensitive data. Conclusion: The Era of AI-Powered Computer Automation Has Arrived Anthropic Enters the "Lobster Farming" arena. This time, Anthropic introduces computer usage functionality, natively implementing computer control within Claude Cowork and Code. Similar to the previously popular OpenClaw, both upgrade AI from simple dialogue interaction to intelligent agents capable of directly manipulating desktops and autonomously executing tasks. The difference lies in that Anthropic's capabilities are native, built upon the official product system, while OpenClaw is merely a third-party open-source implementation. Compared to OpenClaw, which requires users to deploy and debug themselves, Anthropic's new features are ready to use out of the box; users only need to download the client to experience them. Regarding security risks, as an open-source project, OpenClaw not only has a high deployment threshold but also presents significant data and permission security risks; while the official Anthropic solution significantly reduces these risks through mechanisms such as access control and security barriers. Currently, Claude's computer usage functionality is still in an early preview stage, with slow execution speed and not yet available to all users, far from being a replacement for OpenClaw. In the future, when Claude's computer usage functionality is fully launched, coupled with its security barriers and out-of-the-box usability, it may truly "kill OpenClaw."

data.title: Will AI tokens become the new global commodity and currency?
data.description:
On March 23, Liu Liehong, Director of the National Bureau of Data Science and Technology, announced a set of stunning data at the China Development Forum: China's daily AI Token usage has jumped from 100 billion at the beginning of 2024 to 100 trillion by the end of 2025, and is expected to exceed 140 trillion in March 2026, representing a more than 1,000-fold increase in two years. Meanwhile, data from OpenRouter, the world's largest AI model API aggregation platform, shows that China's weekly large-scale model usage has surpassed that of the United States for several consecutive weeks, with Chinese models occupying the top three positions globally. An industrial revolution driven by tokens is reshaping the global technological competition landscape, business models, and even national core competitiveness at an unprecedented speed. In early 2026, several industry developments from Silicon Valley also attracted global attention in the tech world. OpenAI is gradually abandoning DAU (Daily Active Users), a core internet metric used for nearly 20 years, and instead adopting TPD (Token Per Day) as its core operating indicator. This shift is by no means accidental. At GTC 2026, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang redefined data centers as "token factories," pointing out that the core of future competition is "token throughput per watt." This is not an isolated phenomenon, but rather signifies the full arrival of a new paradigm for the intelligent economy, with tokens as the core unit of measurement and transaction. I. The Value and Measurement of AI Tokens 1. AI Tokens as a Value Benchmark in the Intelligent Era From a computer science perspective, tokens are the basic units for AI models to process various types of information. When text is input into a model, it is broken down into words or sub-words; an image is decomposed into pixel blocks; an audio clip is segmented into time segments. These indivisible basic units can all be called tokens. In practical applications, the measurement of tokens follows certain rules. For English text, a short word might count as only one token, while longer words are broken down into multiple tokens; a simple rule of thumb is that one token is roughly equivalent to four English characters. For Chinese text, one Chinese character typically corresponds to one to two tokens. Whether it's data processing during model training or functional output during model service calls, every core action of AI uses tokens as the core measurement scale. The scale of token consumption directly reflects the model's workload and value output, consistent with Marx's labor theory of value. The outstanding contribution of tokens lies in providing a quantifiable and comparable value scale for the development of the intelligent economy. As AI technology evolves from text-based modalities to multimodal iterations and its application scenarios deepen into fields such as programming, video, and scientific research, the strategic positioning of tokens as a "unified unit of measurement" is becoming increasingly prominent. This positioning is not arbitrary but an inevitable result of industrial development: the industrial era needed "kilowatt-hours" to measure electricity consumption, the internet era needed "gigawatt-hours" to measure data flow, and the AI ​​era naturally needs tokens to measure intelligent output. At the economic and commercial level, tokens have become the core value unit of the intelligent era—measurable, priced, and tradable. They connect the underlying energy, computing power, and data with the top-level intelligent services, serving as a universal benchmark for measuring AI productivity, calculating AI costs, and settling AI service payments. The token value chain encompasses five major segments: hardware manufacturing, infrastructure construction, computing power provision, platform operation, and application development. Electricity and computing power depreciation account for as much as 70%-80% of its cost structure, becoming a core element determining the international competitiveness of tokens. "Token throughput per watt" has become a core indicator for measuring the competitiveness of AI companies. This means that, with a fixed electricity budget, whoever can produce more tokens with higher energy efficiency has the lowest production cost and the strongest market competitiveness. 2. Factors Affecting AI Token Measurement With the extreme enrichment of application scenarios, token measurement methods have evolved from simple counting in the early stages to a complex system of multi-dimensional, dynamically weighted metrics. (1) The binary differentiation of input and output. The most basic measurement still follows the binary structure of "input token" and "output token". The input token represents the amount of information provided by the user to the model (including prompts, uploaded documents, historical dialogue records, etc.), while the output token is the response content generated by the model. In commercial billing, because the generation process requires a lot of video memory bandwidth and computing cycles, the cost of the output token is usually 3 to 5 times that of the input token. This price difference reflects the essential difference between "creative labor" and "information reading" in terms of computing power consumption. (2) Context measurement and memory cost. From 2024 to 2025, the context window of large models has experienced a leap from 8K, 32K to 128K and even 1M (millions). In 2026, processing ultra-long contexts has become the norm. However, long contexts are not a free lunch. The attention mechanism based on the Transformer architecture often results in a quadratic or linear increase in computational complexity when processing long sequences. Therefore, modern econometric systems have introduced "context-weighted coefficients". When a user asks a question in a session with 1 million token contexts, even if only 10 tokens are generated as an answer, the system still needs to rescan or retrieve a vast amount of historical memory. This implicit cost is included in the cost of "active context tokens". This makes the econometrics more accurately reflect the resource cost of the model in maintaining long-term memory. (3) Tokenization of multimodal data. With the maturity of multimodal large models (LMM), images, videos and audios have also been incorporated into the token econometrics system. A high-resolution image is no longer considered a single file, but is cut into hundreds of visual patches, each of which is encoded as one or more visual tokens. A one-minute video may be converted into tens of thousands of temporal visual tokens. This unified econometrics breaks down modal barriers, allowing image description, video understanding and voice interaction to be accounted for under the same economic model. For example, generating a 10-second high-definition video might consume the same number of tokens as writing a thousand-word article, which intuitively reflects the difference in information density between different modalities.

(4) The Invisibility of Token Value. With the popularization of AI Agents, models no longer simply provide a single answer, but engage in complex autonomous planning, code execution, self-reflection, and multi-round searches. This process generates a large number of intermediate thinking tokens, which are not directly displayed to users, but are the cornerstone of high-quality output. New measurement standards are beginning to distinguish between "surface output tokens" and "internal reasoning tokens." For highly difficult scientific calculations or complex logical reasoning, the number of internal reasoning tokens may be dozens of times that of the final output. Some advanced platforms have begun to try differentiated billing based on the number of effective reasoning steps or the depth of the thinking chain, marking a fundamental shift in the measurement system from "counting words" to "counting intelligence."

3. The Development Trends of AI Tokens

In recent years, the development of AI tokens has shown three core trends: an exponential explosion in total supply, extreme compression of units, and the stratification and solidification of value.

Trend 1: Explosive Growth in Consumption. According to statistics, the global daily token consumption was approximately 100 billion in 2024, while by the first quarter of 2026, this figure had soared to 180 trillion, an increase of nearly 1800 times. This growth is not a linear accumulation, but rather stems from a qualitative change in application paradigms. Early token consumption mainly came from human-computer dialogue (Chatbots), which is a low-frequency, shallow interaction; while the mainstream application in 2026 is Autonomous Agents. When an Agent performs a task, it will autonomously break down the target, call tools, write and debug code, and verify the results. This closed-loop process may generate the consumption of tens of thousands or even hundreds of thousands of tokens.

In the future, with the implementation of Embodied AI, the perception and decision-making of robots every second will be transformed into a massive real-time token flow. It is estimated that the global daily token consumption will reach the K (10^16) level by 2030. Trend Two: Moore's Law-like decline in unit cost. Thanks to the iteration of hardware architecture (such as the mass production of NVIDIA Blackwell and subsequent Rubin architecture), the optimization of software algorithms (such as hybrid expert models MoE, quantization technology, speculative sampling), and the improvement of cluster scheduling efficiency, the computing cost of generating a high-quality token in 2026 will be about two orders of magnitude lower than in 2023. This "Jeves' paradox" effect is vividly demonstrated in the field of AI: the improvement in efficiency has not reduced the total resource consumption, but has instead stimulated unprecedented demand. In the future, with the introduction of disruptive technologies such as photonic computing and neuromorphic chips, the energy consumption per token is expected to be further reduced, making "infinite intelligence" theoretically possible. Trend Three: Value stratification and specialization. The future token market will exhibit a clear "value stratification." "Standard tokens" generated by general-purpose models will be as cheap and homogeneous as electricity, primarily used for everyday question answering, basic translation, and simple classification; while "high-level tokens," fine-tuned in vertical fields, backed by exclusive private data, and possessing deep reasoning capabilities, will become expensive and scarce. For example, a diagnostic suggestion token generated by a top-tier medical model will be far more valuable than a casual chatbot token. This stratification will give rise to a "token futures market" and a "quality certification system," where users will pay a premium for tokens of specific quality-of-service (QoS) levels.

II. Comparison of the AI ​​Token Industry in China and the United States

1. Production and Consumption Scale: China Achieves Total Overtaking

The core advantages of the United States in the AI ​​field lie in two aspects: chip design and model capabilities.

Nvidia, the undisputed leader in the global GPU market, has seen its market capitalization surge from approximately $300 billion at the end of 2022 to over $4 trillion today, a 14-fold increase. Behind this growth lies the continued leadership of the United States in advanced process chip design. Meanwhile, closed-source models like Claude and GPT are still considered the most powerful, maintaining high prices above $5 per million tokens. This pricing strategy reflects both the technological superiority of US models and their pricing power in the high-end market. However, the US's leading position is facing structural challenges. On the one hand, grid bottlenecks are beginning to restrict the further expansion of AI computing power, with electricity costs remaining high; on the other hand, the dense model's technical route leads to low computing power utilization, making it difficult to rapidly reduce the production cost per token. Unlike the US, China's competitive advantage lies primarily in cost control and its open-source ecosystem. Chinese models like DeepSeek have driven prices down to $0.028 per million tokens, only 1/180th of GPT's price. This exceptional cost-effectiveness is attracting global developers to "vote with their feet"—in the week of February 16-22, 2026, the token consumption of Chinese models on the OpenRouter platform reached 5.16 trillion, a 127% increase compared to three weeks prior, while US models only reached 2.7 trillion and continued to decline. China occupies four of the top five global models, accounting for 85.7% of the total. The weekly usage of Chinese models surpassed that of the US for the first time in February 2026 and has maintained its lead ever since. Domestic models such as MiniMax, DeepSeek, and Kimi have consistently dominated the top spots, and the global token consumption of Chinese models once exceeded 60%. It is important to emphasize that China's surge in token consumption primarily occurred on the inference side, not the training side. Inference has lower requirements for single-card performance, and domestically produced chips with deep optimization are sufficient to support massive inference demands; however, training still relies on a small number of high-end cards and requires distributed architecture and MoE technology to run good models. This structural characteristic means that China has formed a significant advantage in the application and value realization of AI, but there is still room for improvement in the underlying support of basic model innovation. 2. China has cost advantages in energy and engineering. China's cost advantage comes from the synergy of multiple dimensions. Electricity cost is the most fundamental part of token production cost, usually accounting for more than 30% of the total computing power cost. Since AI training and inference are both power-intensive processes, a country's power grid stability and electricity cost (especially green electricity cost) determine the competitiveness of its token production cost. In terms of energy, China's East-West Computing Project and the construction of a unified power grid have enabled the price of green electricity in the west to be as low as 0.2 yuan/kWh, equivalent to about 0.028 USD/kWh, while electricity prices in Europe and the United States are generally in the range of 0.08-0.12 USD/kWh. Chip cost includes hardware procurement cost, depreciation cost, and maintenance cost. The US, leveraging Nvidia's leading position, has an advantage in high-end chip supply, but this also means higher procurement costs. China's strategy is to rely on a small number of high-end chips in the training phase and use domestically produced chips extensively in the inference phase, minimizing the cost per unit of computing power through optimization. At the full-stack collaboration level, Chinese manufacturers have achieved deep integration of models, cloud services, and chips, maximizing computing power utilization, while US manufacturers rely more on third-party cloud and chip providers, resulting in high integration costs. Engineering efficiency is a key variable determining the difference in token costs. At the engineering technology level, Chinese manufacturers have adopted a large-scale MoE (Hybrid Expert) architecture—breaking down large models into multiple experts and activating only a few relevant experts to handle tasks. With the same $1,000 investment in computing power, the number of tokens produced by different technical approaches can differ by more than 10 times. Compared to dense models, the MoE architecture can increase token output per unit of computing power by several times. Full-stack collaborative optimization is equally important—when model vendors, cloud service providers, and chip designers cooperate deeply, the improvement in computing power utilization often exceeds expectations. The global AI competition has shifted from a simple "model performance race" to a comprehensive competition of national power centered on "token production efficiency" and "unit token cost." China, with its cheap and stable energy supply, vast and unified market, and efficient engineering implementation capabilities, has established a significant advantage in the large-scale, low-cost production of tokens, becoming a global "cost haven" and "scale factory" for AI computing power. The United States, on the other hand, relies on original technology, a high-end ecosystem, and financial capital to occupy the high end of the value chain. The essence of this competition is a comprehensive contest of energy pricing power, industrial organization capabilities, and digital ecosystem influence. In the near future, we may see China transform its domestic energy and power advantages into international trade advantages, in addition to traditional industrial products and electronic components, with a highly competitive new commodity—AI tokens. In this rapidly growing field, China has a trade surplus with all countries except the United States, which will reshape the global economic and strategic landscape. III. Will AI Tokens Become a New Global Monetary Asset? 1. The Necessary Conditions for Monetization and the Gap with Reality To explore whether AI Token can become a globally circulating currency, we must first clarify the essential attributes of currency. Economics argues that for an asset to become currency, it needs to fulfill three core functions: a measure of value, a medium of exchange, and a store of value. In addition, it also needs universal acceptance, value stability, and sovereign credit backing. By these standards, AI Token is unlikely to become a true currency in the foreseeable future. Value instability is the biggest obstacle to AI Token as currency. In the past two years, the price per unit of token has fallen by more than 99%. This drastic price fluctuation means that no merchant is willing to accept a "currency" that could be halved in value within a week. Even if prices stabilize in the future, the value of AI Token remains highly tied to computing power costs, which are themselves affected by multiple factors such as chip technology iterations, energy price fluctuations, and geopolitical conflicts, making long-term stability difficult. Insufficient acceptability is another key constraint. Currently, AI Tokens are only accepted for calling APIs and using AI applications, and cannot be used to purchase everyday goods and services. The essence of currency is a general equivalent of various social goods, while the AI ​​Token network is currently limited to the AI ​​service sector. For AI Tokens to gain widespread acceptance, a global network for trading goods and services needs to be built, requiring massive infrastructure investment and long-term market cultivation. Rather than becoming currency, AI Tokens are more likely to evolve into a new type of commodity, similar to traditional commodities such as oil, gold, and copper. This judgment is based on the following observations: First, AI Tokens possess the core characteristics of commodities. Commodities typically have characteristics such as standardization, tradability, and widespread demand, and AI Tokens fully meet these characteristics. Jensen Huang clearly pointed out that "future data centers will become roaring factories operating day and night, producing no longer traditional products, but the most core and valuable commodities of the future digital world: Tokens." Just as the industrial age needed oil as fuel, the intelligent age needs tokens as "intelligent fuel." Secondly, the pricing mechanism of tokens is converging with that of commodities. Currently, the pricing of AI model APIs exhibits clear market characteristics: prices rise when supply is tight and fall when demand is weak. This price formation mechanism is highly similar to traditional commodity markets. As token trading becomes more scaled and standardized, a token derivatives market similar to crude oil futures and gold futures may emerge in the future, providing risk management tools for producers, consumers, and investors. Thirdly, the supply and demand structure of tokens exhibits typical characteristics of commodities. The supply side is constrained by hard constraints such as chip production capacity and power supply, resulting in long expansion cycles and low adjustment elasticity; the demand side grows rapidly with the popularization of AI applications, exhibiting a clear pro-cyclical nature. This supply and demand structure determines that token prices will fluctuate cyclically, rather than declining linearly. In fact, the token price surge in early 2026 has already proven this point—although token prices are trending downwards in the long term, short-term supply and demand imbalances can still trigger price spikes. Fourth, tokens are becoming a potential option for national strategic reserves. As AI capabilities penetrate key areas such as defense, finance, and energy, computing power security has risen to the level of national security. Some countries may begin to strategically reserve computing power resources, and tokens, as a unit of measurement for computing power, naturally become a benchmark for measuring computing power reserves. This trend may give rise to a "computing power-based system"—a new type of reserve system anchored to computing power as value. 2. AI Token Stablecoins Offer New Solutions While AI tokens themselves are unlikely to become currency, a noteworthy trend is that stablecoins are becoming an innovative form of currency for the AI ​​Agent economy. When AI Agents need to make autonomous decisions and trade autonomously, the traditional financial system reveals a series of inadequacies: banks do not open accounts for AI, credit cards are not designed for algorithms, and credit systems are designed for humans. For AI, money is not wealth, but an interface; not a store of value, but a path to execute logic. Against this backdrop, stablecoins on the blockchain exhibit unique advantages—permissionless global transactions, instant settlement, and low-cost collaboration, perfectly aligning with the economic needs of AI Agents. Data shows that the application of stablecoins in the AI ​​Agent economy is growing rapidly. As of March 2026, the global x402 ecosystem had exceeded 163 million transactions, with a total transaction volume exceeding $45 million, the number of buyer AI Agents exceeding 435,000, and the number of seller AI Agents exceeding 90,000. Among them, USDC holds an absolute dominant position in the x402 protocol transaction layer, accounting for 98.6% of transaction volume on the EVM chain and 99.7% on the Solana chain. 3. Three Possible Paths for Future Evolution Based on the above analysis, the future evolution of AI Tokens may present three paths: Path 1: Maintaining its unit of measurement positioning, not becoming an independent asset. In this scenario, AI Tokens always serve as the unit of account for AI services, but do not possess independent asset attributes. Users are purchasing AI capabilities, not the Token itself; the Token is merely a means of payment, not an investment target. This is the most conservative prediction and the current reality. Path Two: Evolving into a major asset, forming a computing power futures market. As the scale of Token trading expands and standardization increases, Tokens may become tradable commodities like oil and copper. Exchanges will launch Token futures, options, and other derivatives, providing market participants with price discovery and risk management tools. Under this path, Token price fluctuations will be more volatile, but also more financially oriented. Path Three: As a measurement benchmark for a computing power-based system, supporting a new monetary system. This is the most revolutionary path: computing power becomes the value anchor of currency, similar to the role of gold in the gold standard. In this system, digital currencies (CBDCs) issued by sovereign states use computing power as the value benchmark, with each unit of currency corresponding to a standardized number of Tokens. This path faces enormous technological and institutional challenges, but if realized, it will completely reshape the global monetary system.

IV. Strategies for Coping with the AI ​​Token Era

1. Strengthening Computing Power Sovereignty and Strategic Infrastructure Construction at the National Level

Faced with the rise of the token economy, the nation needs to incorporate computing power resources into its strategic infrastructure planning and proactively consider the governance issues of the token economy. Specifically, this can be approached from the following dimensions:

Constructing a computing power infrastructure system. Drawing on the successful experience of the "Eastern Data, Western Computing" project, a nationwide computing power network should be planned in a coordinated manner to promote the efficient allocation of computing power resources. This includes: deploying large-scale intelligent computing centers in the energy-rich western regions to leverage green electricity to reduce computing power costs; building edge computing nodes in the demand-intensive eastern regions to ensure low-latency service capabilities; and constructing a unified national computing power scheduling platform to achieve on-demand allocation and flexible scheduling of computing power resources.

Promote the unification of token measurement standards. Currently, the token measurement methods used by various platforms are diverse, causing inconvenience for developers in selecting tokens and for enterprises in cost accounting, and also restricting the large-scale development of the token economy. At the national level, industry associations and leading enterprises can be guided to jointly formulate token measurement standards, clarify the token conversion rules for different modalities (text, image, audio), and establish a transparent and fair token cost accounting mechanism. This will not only benefit the efficient operation of the domestic market, but also help enhance China's voice in the global token economy. Improve the token economy governance framework. The rapid development of the token economy brings a series of new governance challenges: How to define the legal attributes of tokens (service measurement unit, digital asset, or security)? How to regulate cross-border token transactions? How to prevent financial risks caused by token price fluctuations? How to strike a balance between protecting user rights and encouraging innovation? Answering these questions requires close collaboration between policymakers, technology experts, industry, and academia to build a governance system adapted to the characteristics of the intelligent economy. Strengthen participation in international rules. The current global AI governance rules are in their formative stage, and China should actively participate in the formulation of international token economy rules. This includes: promoting international standards for token measurement within a multilateral framework, incorporating computing power cooperation clauses into bilateral trade agreements, and proposing token transaction tax schemes that align with the interests of developing countries in digital tax negotiations. Only by mastering the power to formulate rules can China take the initiative in the future global token economy landscape. 2. Enterprises Need to Reconstruct Token Efficiency Thinking and Business Models For enterprises, token strategy is no longer simply a technical strategy, but a top-level design that influences core competitiveness and business value. Faced with the token economy wave, enterprises need to re-examine their strategies from the following dimensions: Establish a token efficiency mindset. When selecting AI technologies, enterprises should incorporate token efficiency into core evaluation criteria, focusing on the matching degree between computing power resources and token consumption. From prompt word design and model calling strategies to result optimization, every step must consider both efficiency and cost. Precise design of prompts can reduce invalid token consumption, and reasonable model invocation strategies can improve computing power utilization. These details directly affect the final cost of an enterprise's AI investment. Drawing on the "good-put" concept from the communications field, enterprises need to focus on "how many tokens truly drive user goals to be achieved," rather than simply pursuing token throughput. The core of this shift in thinking lies in moving from "how much computing power was used" to "how much value was created." Reconstructing business models and pricing strategies. The large-scale model industry is undergoing a transformation from "traffic subsidies" to "value screening." Early low-price strategies attracted a large number of trial-and-error users, resulting in inefficient use of computing resources—some vendors have reported that 40% of their free quota consumption comes from test calls without actual business scenarios. By appropriately raising prices, enterprises can filter out non-essential needs while ensuring service stability for high-quality customers. This refined operation of "compensating for volume with price" signifies that the industry is moving from internet-style scale expansion to value-based pricing in the software industry. Establishing new talent standards and incentive mechanisms. At the 2026 GTC conference, Jensen Huang proposed a forward-thinking idea: to issue engineers a token budget equivalent to half their annual salary as an incentive to attract talent. He even explicitly stated, "If you hire a software engineer with an annual salary of $500,000, and he hasn't consumed at least $250,000 worth of tokens, I would be deeply concerned." 3. Cultivating Token Literacy and New Human-Machine Collaboration Capabilities at the Individual Level For individuals, the rise of the token economy presents both challenges and opportunities. Faced with this profound productivity revolution, individuals need to build a new capability structure from the following dimensions: Establishing Token Literacy. The vast majority of users lack sufficient understanding of token consumption, model capabilities, and pricing mechanisms, leading to various problems when using AI—some people use AI agents to buy and sell stocks, only to wake up to find their accounts empty overnight; others post on social media instructing all AI agents to hand over API interfaces, causing multiple agents to be "tricked." These cases warn us that token literacy is becoming a fundamental capability in the digital age. Building a new way of working through human-machine collaboration. Jensen Huang predicts that in the future, computers will operate 24/7 and continuously generate tokens as AI agents tirelessly execute tasks. This means that individuals need to shift their work style from "doing it themselves" to "directing AI to do it," from "executor" to "supervisor." Embracing lifelong learning and skill iteration. The rapid development of the token economy means that the half-life of skills is shortening. Today's mainstream models may soon be replaced by new optimization technologies; current popular models will soon be surpassed by more efficient architectures. In this environment, maintaining learning and adaptability is more important than mastering specific skills. Therefore, individuals need to cultivate a habit of continuous learning, pay attention to the latest developments in AI technology and the token economy; proactively try new tools and methods, accumulating experience in practice; and build an interdisciplinary knowledge structure to understand the economic logic and social impact behind the technology. Only in this way can one remain invincible in the wave of the token economy.

data.title: Can Balancer emerge from its darkest hour by making a tough decision?
data.description:
Author: KarenZ, Foresight News

On November 3, 2025, a security incident that resulted in losses exceeding $120 million shattered the illusion of growth for Balancer, a veteran DeFi protocol.

This was the largest security incident in Balancer's history. But the deeper damage lies not in those astronomical figures.

Looking at the financial data attached to Balancer's latest proposal, its fundamentals are already far from optimistic: the protocol's annualized fees are approximately $1.65 million, while the DAO's estimated annualized revenue is only $290,000, accounting for 17.5%.

The remaining funds flowed to veBAL holders, the core pool, the Balancer Alliance plan, and other parties.

The entire system appears to be a continuously operating "money printing machine," but it's actually leaking on two sides: First, transaction fees are lost through multiple layers of distribution; second, the annual inflation of BAL tokens releases approximately 3.78 million tokens, creating a continuous selling pressure of about $580,000 at the current price—considering that BAL's current fully diluted valuation (FDV) is only $11 million. The annual operating budget is a staggering $2.87 million, while annualized revenue is only $290,000, resulting in a shortfall of $2.58 million. The DAO treasury (excluding BAL) has only $10.3 million left. At this rate, the treasury has less than four years to live. Following the security incident, Balancer TVL suffered even more. Balancer TVL plummeted from $800 million to approximately $300 million, and has continued to decrease, currently standing at less than $160 million. It's worth noting that at its peak in 2021, Balancer's TVL (TVL) exceeded $3 billion. Balancer has officially reached a crossroads. On March 23, 2026, the Balancer core team simultaneously released two important governance proposals: a comprehensive reform of the BAL token economics and a restructuring of the operational architecture.
